title: Born to Leroy and Leo Vester
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Library of Congress on December 11, 2015, Reginald Robinson opens by recounting his birth in Baltimore in 1939 to Leroy and Leo Vester Robinson, his father's thirty-nine years at Bethlehem Steel and death a month after retiring, his mother's piecework, and his two older siblings Leroy and Lula Jean.

title: The Streets Won
summary: Robinson tells how he left Dunbar High in the tenth grade for the streets until a job fell apart, and how his cousin Josephine Thorn convinced his father to give him another chance at Cortez Peters Business College, founded by a Black man who could type 150 words a minute and taught Black students to pass government tests.

title: When Things Broke Loose in Sixty
summary: After the Greensboro sit-ins of 1960, Robinson describes the student Civic Interest Group forming in Baltimore, his school dean and city councilman Walter Dixon ordering him to represent the school at the big meetings, meeting students from Maryland colleges with Morgan in the lead, and moving from direct action toward voter registration.

title: The Seat From Maryland
topic: Becoming Treasurer and Taking the SNCC Seat
summary: Robinson recounts becoming treasurer of the Civic Interest Group through his accounting background, registering some thirty thousand people in Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s area, and how, when the Mitchell family's NAACP would no longer bail the group and Clarence stepped aside, he took the open Maryland seat at SNCC, arriving with a black patch over an eye hit by a pretzel.

title: Son, I Know Who You Are
summary: At the June 1961 SNCC meeting in Louisville, Kentucky, where everyone assumed his eye patch was a battle injury, Robinson describes standing on Anne Braden's porch with a beer when Ella Baker walked up, traced his family through Smithfield, Virginia and his mother Leo Vester, declared he was just like his uncle McKinley who worked with her in New York, and gave him standing in the group.

title: The Collection Before the Preacher
summary: Robinson tells of the big rally at the Baltimore Masonic Temple where King came to convince the students not to go South, and how he arranged to take the collection before Wyatt Tee Walker and the preachers arrived, splitting the proceeds between SNCC and the Civic Interest Group before Walker could call for it himself.

title: They Thought I Was an Expert
summary: Robinson explains that his Baltimore voter registration, sound trucks and school buses and big parties that signed up thousands, made the group think him an expert, so he was elected to join Bob Moses in Mississippi, reasoning his life was in as much jeopardy in Baltimore and choosing to be productive in the South.

title: Top Hat and Tails in Atlanta
topic: Ed King and a Detour to McComb
summary: Robinson recalls reaching Atlanta to change trains, met by executive director Ed King in top hat and tails after integrating the National Symphony the night before, being shown around Auburn Avenue near King's SCLC office where Wyatt Tee Walker tried to recruit him, and getting word to meet Bob Moses in McComb rather than the Delta.

title: Super Cool Daddy
summary: Robinson describes arriving in McComb, met by local cab driver George Hitz and welcomed by C.C. Bryant and Bob Moses, then walking off with Webb Owens, known as Super Cool Daddy, to arrange housing at Mama Cotton's, a deal at a local restaurant, a SNCC bank account, and a rented mimeograph machine.

title: Write That, Read That
summary: Robinson recounts his fear canvassing the projects near white workmen in a sewer hole, and the school they set up over Burglund Supermarket to teach people to read and interpret the Mississippi constitution, since a registrar could flip to any page and fail an applicant whose interpretation he disliked.

title: Build Something and Leave
topic: Herbert Lee and Ella Baker's Method
summary: Robinson tells of walking applicants to the registrar, Bob Moses being beaten, Herbert Lee killed in the cotton gin by State Representative E.H. Hurst, and the lesson from his training rooted in Ella Baker, that organizers go in to duplicate themselves and leave something behind, working themselves out of a job rather than making a home.

title: I Got Elected to Go to the Jail
summary: Robinson recounts the students Curtis Hayes, Hollis Watkins, Bobby Talbert and Brenda Travis getting arrested at the bus station, and being chosen to visit them in jail at Magnolia, riding down with George Hitz, who pulled out a silver-handled pistol, while Robinson, dressed like a lawyer, faced a tobacco-chewing jailer.

title: Have Your Black Ass Get Out of Here
summary: Robinson describes the doors slamming behind him as he climbed to the cell, finally seeing Curtis and Hollis, calming them and relaying the jailer's words, then calling the jailer to be let out and reporting to Julian Bond that everyone was fine, while noting he could not see Brenda Travis and the girls.

title: East Baltimore, Strike One
summary: Robinson backtracks to Baltimore, where coming from East Baltimore among upper-class West Baltimore students was a strike against him, and how he came to coordinate the demonstrations, piling seven or eight people into each of three Renaults to hit the biggest restaurants at lunch and dodge arrest after the police read the Miranda warning.

title: Little Willie Came to the Rescue
summary: Robinson names the businessman William Little Willie Adams, who helped with bond when the NAACP argued the public accommodations bill was already in the courts, and the union representative Troy Brailey, who knew his father, sat on the students' advisory committee, and helped him into the treasurer's stipend.

title: Soothing My Mother
summary: Robinson credits Elizabeth Murphy Oliver and her daughter Marcia of the Baltimore Afro-American newspaper family for introducing him around and the Jet and Ebony stringer Larry Steele, who on his runs through Baltimore would stop to reassure Robinson's mother he was safe, and reflects on his family's reactions, his proud father, his uncertain brother, and his own resolve that if he survived where he was going he would be all right the rest of his life.

part: The Walkout and the Eastern Shore
title: The Sound Getting Louder
summary: Returning to McComb, Robinson describes the day the students walked out of school as the worst, with a warrant out for Diane Nash and Bob Moses begged not to come in, the sixteen of them singing and praying, then breaking out the signs and marching the students into town while Robinson took up an observer's post to phone reports to Julian Bond.

part: The Walkout and the Eastern Shore
title: Through the Black Space
topic: Escape Across the Masonic Temple Roof
summary: Robinson recounts the violence breaking loose downtown, the police beating Bob Moses and picking up Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon, and how, watching from the Masonic Temple as a big sergeant climbed the stairs, he led Diane Nash and a man named Gaston through a black cloth onto the roof, where Gaston leapt off and was caught on the highway while Robinson got Diane Nash safely back inside.

part: The Walkout and the Eastern Shore
title: No Room in the Inn
topic: The Crisfield Sit-In and His Only Arrest
summary: Robinson tells how, McComb too hot, the group regrouped at B.B. Beamon's in Atlanta and divided the work before a call from Ruby Doris Smith Robinson sent him to the CORE office over untested New York volunteers, Faith Holsaert, Peggy Dammond, Angela Butler and others joined by Bill Hansen, who staged a Christmas 1961 sit-in at Governor Tawes's restaurant in Crisfield, Maryland, where the police arrested them, the only time in six years Robinson was locked up.

title: We Build, We Grow, We Leave
summary: Robinson recounts being jailed in Princess Anne with the Mitchell family as attorneys, the bondsmen Freddie St. Clair and Walter Black, and the group deciding on Freddie St. Clair's invitation to take a look at Cambridge, where weekend demonstrations grew as the community joined, the SNCC rule was to build then leave, and St. Clair gave up the organization to his cousin Gloria Richardson, of an old Cambridge family.

title: You Cannot Stay Out of the Vote
summary: Robinson recounts clashing with Gloria Richardson when the white establishment called a referendum on public accommodations and she urged Black residents not to vote, his argument that a SNCC affiliate could not advocate no vote while people died for the vote in the South, and the Cambridge board siding with him.

title: Get Your Man in Check
summary: Sent with Bill Hansen to talk up a conference on the Black and white campuses, Robinson reflects on the white organizer he felt was hiding from his own community, his arrogant manner that drove Sal Mineo from a New York party with people like Godfrey Cambridge, and his announcing their movements aloud as they slipped into Cambridge at night.

title: The Twelve High
summary: Robinson tells how Ruby Doris split them up, Hansen to the white campuses and himself to the Black ones, his being smuggled onto a campus for two weeks, scouting the Air Force Academy area, and meeting twelve ministers they called the twelve high and Amelia Boynton, a connection that led to Bernard Lafayette being sent to Selma.

title: A White Boy in the March
topic: Why They Held Bob Moses Back
summary: Asked to explain the McComb march, Robinson recounts the group's reasoning that a white man visible in the march would make it far rougher, contrasting Bob Moses, who moved openly with his community and was not hiding, with Bill Hansen, and how Moses insisting on coming aggravated the police, who beat him.

title: Amzie Convinced Us to Stay
summary: Robinson describes how the young workers' first instinct after the killings was to run, until Amzie Moore counseled them to show they were unafraid, turning a funeral into a mass meeting where Bob Moses and McDew spoke and Robinson was assigned to photograph Louis Allen, who was later killed, and how James Forman's arrival organized SNCC.

title: He Kept Trying to Find His Freedom
summary: Robinson portrays Amzie Moore as a military veteran and postal worker who could not abide what he found back in Mississippi, joined the NAACP, and organized fearlessly, and explains how Moore, C.C. Bryant, Webb Owens, Aaron Henry and others were a network already built, so the workers walked into something needing only organizers, with McComb chosen over a harder county.

title: How Direct Is a Crack on the Head
summary: Robinson reflects on the 1961 SNCC debate between direct action and voter registration, saying he saw little difference since both got you beaten, and on confronting the National Guard in Cambridge, which made the accommodations fight there more intense than Mississippi, while Bobby Kennedy was the same adversary in McComb, Mississippi and Cambridge.

title: Watch Out, the White Folks Might Turn Green
topic: John Doar and Affidavits in the Dark
summary: Robinson recalls Robert Kennedy noting that John Doar had spoken of him from Mississippi, the night Bob Moses, John Doar, Marion Barry, George Hitz and he went to a farmer's house in an open field for a registration affidavit as the old man feared white folks might turn green, and how John Hardy, badly beaten at Walthall, was hurt that Doar pretended not to know him in the cell block, though Doar became a friend who later brought a Justice Department man to calm Cambridge with the profane Pop Herb St. Clair.

title: A Penny Piece of Money
summary: Robinson explains that after Mississippi and Alabama were funded a little Voter Education Project money was left for Orangeburg, South Carolina, where the NAACP wanted no SNCC people until Randolph Blackwell and Dr. Thomas at South Carolina State arranged for him to come in only to match registered voters to precinct captains, conferring with the state representative Pete Bryant while staying off Claflin's campus, a cafeteria demonstration breaking out when he was out of town, before he was sent to support Dottie Burlage's Northern Student Movement project in Raleigh.

title: They Sent In George Gelston
summary: Robinson describes Cambridge shut down and the Eastern Shore's mayor upset, the National Guard sent in under General George Gelston to quell things, and how Gelston became an ally, meeting with Robinson, Gloria Richardson and lawyer Fred Weisgal in Baltimore, even though Robinson warned the Guard would only make things worse.

title: They Inkblots
summary: Robinson recounts telling General Gelston he was classified four-F and mentally incompetent, and the story of his draft board exam, where he insisted to the sergeant that the inkblots were only inkblots, reasoning that if the army told him they were inkblots he would believe they were inkblots, which got him classified incompetent.

title: Muster Gas Over the Town
topic: Tear Gas and the Tawes Nephew
summary: Robinson tells how Gelston would let a kneel-and-pray demonstration pass and hold his troops, but one night when Gelston was away and his deputy Colonel Tawes commanded, Cliff Vaughs charged the young soldiers, the troops fired tear gas over the Black section, Courtland and Stokely were arrested, and Robinson helped people hit by canisters before getting everyone out.

title: Fire Returned
summary: Robinson recounts negotiating over the absence of Black soldiers in the Guard until a Black colonel from Baltimore arrived and it was integrated, the Guard then observing the negotiations, and how before Gelston the town nearly erupted, with gunfire across an open field between the Black and white communities answered shot for shot.

title: Promises and a Strong Community
summary: Robinson reflects that Cambridge's fight was about jobs and access like later Northern movements, with factories closing and watermen idle, many promises made by figures like Robert C. Weaver at HUD that he is unsure were ever kept, but a strong community left behind, which was always the purpose.

title: She Followed the People
summary: Robinson describes Gloria Richardson as a leader who took direction from her board and the demands of the people before later running with the New York act group, crediting her and the St. Clair name with making the Cambridge movement, and explaining the NAACP's stance that with cases in court there was no need to spend money bailing out demonstrators.

title: Building Clarences
summary: Robinson explains that pushing the envelope when the NAACP thought them finished kept the movement alive until Henry G. Parks Jr. and others backed them, and recalls how voter registration's records required a secretary, Norma, who came up through Clarence Logan and went to Atlanta to work, in an office so small the early staff could barely fit.

title: The Adult Crowd
summary: Robinson recounts knowing the historian August Meier, a Morgan teacher close to the students, and the adult advisory group including Reverend Bascom, Reverend Vernon Dobson, Reverend Frank Williams and a Miss Adams, whose advice was not always taken, though their churches and approval were needed to release funds.

title: They Bumped Our Speech
summary: Robinson says he attended the March on Washington only to show up and be with his group, singing and eating, irritated that the organizers had already cut what the students wanted to say, his view of the power game then as now, and describes Judy Richardson emerging beside him in Cambridge as someone who simply came along and stuck.

title: The Outsider Who Could Unite Them
summary: Robinson describes the competition among NAG, Nashville and Atlanta hubs over leadership and nonviolence, contrasting Chuck McDew of Massillon, Ohio, whom one could not easily convert to nonviolence, with the divinity-minded Bernard Lafayette under Jim Lawson, and how the two could still act together under the nonviolent banner.

title: He Didn't Want It
summary: Robinson recounts taking McDew as his chairman and becoming close brothers, and his understanding that Ella Baker favored McDew for chair precisely because he was not lobbying for it and stood outside the school rivalries that would have stalled her vision, an outsider, like Robinson himself, who could bring the competing groups together.

title: A Doodle of Hearts
summary: Robinson tells of first sitting across from Diane Nash at a conference, transfixed by the most beautiful woman he had seen as he stared with his eye patch, and how Connie Curry later teased him about the heart he had doodled, until Ella Baker drew him in and he and Nash became friends, with Robinson later assigned to protect her during the McComb warrant.

title: Beauty in All the Other Ways
summary: Robinson praises the brave and beautiful women of the movement, Diane Nash going to jail while pregnant, Grace Brooks leaving South Carolina State for the Free Southern Theater, and a woman who married Bill Withers, insisting that beauty was not only facial but in all the ways the sisters gave themselves.

title: We Lived in a Whole House
summary: Robinson names more of the women, Judy, Bobbi Yancy, Ruby Doris, Jessie Johnson who handled the money, Connie Curry and Casey Hayden, and recalls that, glad for anywhere to stay when no one else would take them, the workers once lived in what they only later understood was a whorehouse.

title: It Was the Underworld
topic: Who Led Cambridge and What Remains
summary: Robinson observes that in Cambridge it was the men always being jailed, the pool-room crowd, who first joined the demonstrations because they were unafraid and had nothing to lose, and reflects that he cannot say people are happy there or in Mississippi today, where Black officials are many but production and businesses on Pine Street are gone.

title: A Bank on the Corner
topic: An Economic Program for the Community
summary: Robinson lays out his recurring dream of a financial institution on a corner of a small town like Greenwood that would seed a laundromat, a general store, a filling station and a circuit-board factory, keeping money moving in the community, and jokes that he has had no money since SNCC paid him nine dollars and sixty-four cents yet counts himself rich.

title: John's First Campaign
summary: Robinson recalls working John Lewis's first campaign and the question of who ran against Julian Bond, then turns to leaving SNCC and the many directions people scattered, naming the Deacons of Defense work and Lowndes County, as he sets up his own post-movement path.

title: You Boys
summary: Robinson recounts leaving SNCC in 1965 to be McDew's second at a United Planning Organization center in DC, then Ed Brown bringing him to the Child Development Group of Mississippi as social service director, where he was fired after challenging Senator John C. Stennis for prefacing every remark with you boys.

title: A Way Into the Panthers
summary: Robinson describes joining an organization run by Peggy Dammond's husband on housing projects in Detroit, then Kaiser Engineers' forty-year plan to acquire and build on land in East Oakland with the Black Panthers, his being sent to reach them through his ties to Stokely, and the Oakland Panthers refusing him for arriving in a Kaiser Cadillac.

title: West Project Equal
summary: Robinson tells how, rejected by the Panthers, he switched the deal to a community group through Tim Jenkins's contact Don Warden, assembling local people who partnered with Kaiser to acquire land for the senior housing he says now stands as West Project Equal, and how on Kaiser's later model-cities contracts he was always sent to address the Black community but barred from the white meetings, and told he did not need to know, until he was out on the street again.

title: Six Hours of Sleep
topic: The Pills and a Hard Choice
summary: Married and suffering sharp back pains, Robinson describes a doctor prescribing pills that required two hours of sleep three times a day, and how, sitting in his chair with Jack Daniels and a joint, he reasoned he could not sleep six hours and support his family, and began instead to examine the habits weighing on him.

title: Up Three to Nothing
summary: Robinson recounts quitting cigarettes after a friend told him three days would make it, then coffee, which he had drunk before every bus, train and phone call, switching to tea, while feeling trapped working jobs he did not like and bound, like his father before him, to take care of family first.

title: The Job and the Marriage
topic: Western University and a Failing Marriage
summary: Robinson describes leaving OSTI for a welfare-program school running kindergarten through high school for grown women and derelict men, his marriage to a psychiatric social worker straining over their agreement that once she finished her master's she would support his turn to school, an agreement coming due as everything frayed.

title: All My Records and My Chair
summary: Robinson tells of a Bobbi Yancy call to a Memphis Black caucus dinner project, coming home to find his wife leaving as he had once told her she was free to, asking her to wait six weeks, then going on to the project for A.L. Nellum and Associates and, on returning to Boston, deciding to leave for good, settling in DC since 1981.

title: The Only Computer on the Ground
summary: Robinson recounts surviving in DC on consultancies and loans, getting into the charter with John A. Wilson, and joining a computer company, Afro-American Data Namix at Fourteenth and Kenyon, with a DEC PDP-11/45 that ran Marion Barry's first poll, until the electric bills outran the contracts and the lights went out.

title: Forty Thousand in a Summer
topic: The Summer Youth Program and Retirement
summary: Robinson describes joining Marion Barry's summer youth program, matching forty thousand young people to jobs one summer after a disastrous first try, then working as an unemployment-insurance investigator during the shift from paper to data, rising to assistant LAN administrator over a hundred machines before leaving government in May 2001.

title: The Red Comma FBP
summary: Robinson recounts walking out of government the day an incoming administration herded busy workers to hear a paid incentive speaker, filing his papers at sixty-two exactly forty years after starting in May 1961, and signing his emails Robinson comma FBP, a free Black person who does anything he wants and counts himself rich though he has no money.

title: Fundraising Through Sixty-Four
topic: The Tydings Job and the MFDP
summary: Asked what he did in 1964, Robinson recalls leaving to work for Senator Joseph Tydings with Ella Baker's blessing, lobbying for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in California and Oregon, and being sent to Atlantic City to find housing, where he and Mary Lovelace O'Neal of NAG secured hotel rooms through one of the city's biggest gangsters, then fundraising in New York and Philadelphia until a man named Fred Mealy undid the Philly gains and Forman pulled him toward Texas as 1965 fell into disarray.

title: Call Me the Pickup Man
summary: Robinson describes his role as the advance and pickup man who, when reports stopped coming from the field, drove with Jim Forman through Mississippi, Georgia and Alabama, even checking the Deacons of Defense in North Carolina, and characterizes Forman as a matter-of-fact military thinker who would give you the plan and your spot in it.

title: Ride the Bus Up Front
summary: Robinson recounts staying out of the philosophical talk of Fanon and Douglass to focus on strategy, and his Freedom Ride work, helping arrange for riders like Gwen Green, Travis Britt and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson to come down to McComb for voter registration, and his own assignment to ride the bus up front when Montgomery was opened.

title: A Charm to Work With
summary: Robinson explains his unease that Bill Hansen announced their moves aloud when entering Cambridge by night through a dark bus stop, notes Hansen later becoming a professor in Nigeria, and concedes that Hansen, for all that, was knowledgeable about organization, someone you could walk the line with in the struggle.

title: Not the Time to Die for a Piss
topic: Forman and the Gun in Albany
summary: In Albany, Robinson recalls Jim Forman returning to the car still needing the bathroom because a man inside had a gun, joking he would not give his life for the revolution just to take a piss, and Robinson's own assignment hanging around Dr. Anderson's house listening as King's people came in.

title: Old Poke Chops
summary: Robinson describes Wyatt Tee Walker driving him to the Albany jail to deliver a tray, the deal that only movement food reached King and Abernathy, the two in silk pajamas, King thanking him and eating his pork chops while Abernathy, whom Robinson called Old Poke Chops, asked where the ice cream was, before Robinson went back to follow Charles Sherrod's orders in Baker and Terrell.

title: The First SNCC Worker in Selma
summary: Robinson recounts Bob Moses taking him back to Selma years later for the Jubilee, introducing him as the first SNCC worker to land there, and describes Moses's organization the Trail and the Tent City on Highway 80 in Lowndes County, whose records Moses's family still withholds as he wished.

title: Ella and My Mother
topic: A Family Bond With Ella Baker
summary: Robinson describes how Ella Baker became a close mother figure through her bond with his own mother, who fixed lunch for Baker and Fannie Lou Hamer in DC, and how the night before his wedding Ella Baker and Joanne Grant took him out and got him drunk, before he married at Harry Belafonte's house with Baker and his mother as his support.

title: Naked in the Mountain Stream
summary: Robinson tells how, worn down in Cambridge, Ella Baker sent him to the psychiatrist Joe Baker of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, who sent him to rest a week in Peekskill, New York around a Paul Robeson fundraiser, where he was taken to the home of a doctor who had gone to Russia for Robeson and sat naked in a sauna and an icy mountain stream beside Paul Robeson himself.

title: Denim Closer to the People
topic: Chasing the Silk Suits Out of Cambridge
summary: Robinson recounts planting a story with a reporter contrasting organizers in denim who worked close to local people with outsiders in shiny silk suits who came to make collections, which the Cambridge community traced to the NAACP and ran them out of town, and reflects that SNCC was making good on Cambridge without needing to control it like a big organization.

title: One Side Junkies, One Side Millions
topic: Baltimore, Freddie Gray, and the Police
summary: Robinson connects the Freddie Gray unrest to Baltimore he grew up in, describing a Pikesville street with a multimillion-dollar operation on one side and junkies on the other, arguing nothing changes while people are out of work, that the police are part of the power that controls, and that taking away people's income only makes them fight.

title: We Were Not Talking About Guns
topic: Voter Registration, a Black President, and Black Power
summary: Robinson argues that voter registration produced only leaders bound by the same rules and that he felt no different when a Black president came, citing a Black man arrested entering his own house, and contends the move toward Black power was about money and opportunity, schools and recreation, not guns, distorted by a media that latched onto the Panthers' rifles, insisting a funded economic base is the real way to fight.

title: They Are the Leaders
summary: Asked what people should know about the movement, Robinson credits Ella Baker's simple plan of taking people from where they are and lifting them, those on top supporting those at the bottom, and the lesson that there is no one leader but that anyone who rises to do something becomes a leader and gathers followers, so they need no other Martin Luther King.

title: Martin Ain't Drove No Damn Way
summary: Robinson criticizes the film Selma for inventing scenes of Joseph Rauh reprimanding Forman and of John riding with King driving, when Forman had his own organization and standing and King did not drive, praising instead Freedom Song for showing organizers registering people, and insisting John Lewis chose to leave SNCC and was not put out.

title: Coming Back to Organize
summary: Robinson values the SNCC Legacy Project for letting the organizers tell their own story against efforts to rewrite or erase them, says he answered a call that he is available to go to Selma or Ferguson and do whatever is needed short of his piano lessons and fishing, and returns to his dream of an honest, gutsy pooling of Black resources for the community.

title: Henry Parks and the Policy Book
topic: A Sausage Fortune and a Closing
summary: Robinson holds up Henry G. Parks Jr., the hidden Black owner of Parks Sausage Company and the Royal Theatre on the Chitlin' Circuit who moved money through the community from a policy book, as proof that seven or eight pooling their money could seed a bank, a laundromat and a factory in a small town and tell the Klan they had enough to do it, then closes by noting outside buyers would only bring more economics, thanking the interviewers, and restating his policy of granting interviews only in the context of the Legacy Project, vouched for through Charlie.
